What's an API? And What's a RESTful API?
RESTful API is a phrase frontend devs hear constantly. So what is it actually? Let's talk.
First — what's an API. API stands for
Think about Google Maps — you type in a destination, and it shows you a route or location. Or searching for articles or info: you type a keyword in the search box, hit Search, and results appear.
All those experiences depend on APIs. Search "wiki what is an API" and you'll find Wikipedia's explanation:

An application programming interface (API) is a way for two or more computer programs to communicate with each other. It is a type of software interface, offering a service to other pieces of software. A document or standard that describes how to build such a connection or interface is called an API specification.
After reading that, you probably still want a clearer answer.
Let me try a different framing.
SPA, CSR, SSR, MPA, SSG — A Quick Take on Frontend Terminology
Say I (the user) want to look at a description of Godzilla. But the painter (server) is dirt poor, so every time I want something I have to send him a blank piece of paper

a letter (the request) describing what I want — like: "Hi painter, please draw me a picture of Godzilla."
Then I hand the letter to the friendly postman (the network), who delivers it to the painter (server). The painter reads the request, grabs his brushes, paints Godzilla on the blank paper I sent, and the postman delivers the painting back to me.
Same idea as snail mail — you want to send your friend a letter, the postman delivers based on the address, and your friend writes back based on the contents.
Clearer now? In short, an API is
"you ask it to do something, and the system responds based on what you asked"

Design with versioning and extensibility in mind
REST exists to make API design consistent and extensible. For consumers, the endpoint shape alone tells you what it does. So picking up REST conventions even at a basic level is genuinely useful.

RESTful API is a phrase you hear constantly as a frontend dev. But what's actually behind it? This post walks through what an API is (with a postman + painter analogy), then builds up to RESTful API as a design style — the conventions around plural vs. singular resource paths, HTTP methods, URL casing, versioning, and why using REST conventions makes your code easier for other devs to read.
